image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the sample is intended to rely on the diagram.
The question uses spatial/visual relations such as rail inclination, rod orientation, and magnetic field direction relative to the rail plane.
Determining force directions on the rod is easier and likely dependent on the depicted geometry; one registered image exists and registry_passed=true.
multi_step: The problem has two linked sub-questions: first solve for the magnetic field magnitude/direction from equilibrium, then change the field orientation and solve for an external force.
Solving requires combining multiple conditions: gravity component along incline, magnetic force on a current-carrying rod, and direction analysis.
The second part depends on the setup and inferred relations from the first part, indicating multi-step physical reasoning rather than single lookup.
verifiability: The expected answer is a small set of concrete outputs: magnetic field magnitude/direction and external force magnitude/direction.
These quantities can be checked against standard equations for equilibrium of a rod on inclined rails in a magnetic field.
answer_present=true, registry_passed=true, and the provided answer text contains explicit numeric values and directions.

sentence_segments.text: Two parallel, smooth inclined metal rails are spaced $L=0.2\mathrm{m}$ apart, with an angle of $\theta=37^{\circ}$ to the horizontal plane.
A metal rod ab with a mass of $m=0.2\mathrm{kg}$ is placed perpendicularly on the rails.
A uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the rails.
When a current $I=5\mathrm{A}$ flows from b to a through the rod, the rod can remain stationary on the inclined plane.
Determine the direction and magnitude of the magnetic induction $B$ of a uniform magnetic field

If all other conditions remain unchanged, but the direction of the magnetic field is suddenly changed to vertically upward, and an external force $F$ is applied parallel to the rail direction to keep the metal rod stationary, determine the direction and magnitude of the external force $F$ acting on the metal rod.

conditions.text: Two parallel, smooth inclined metal rails are spaced $L=0.2\mathrm{m}$ apart, with an angle of $\theta=37^{\circ}$ to the horizontal plane.
A metal rod ab with a mass of $m=0.2\mathrm{kg}$ is placed perpendicularly on the rails.
A uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the rails.
When a current $I=5\mathrm{A}$ flows from b to a through the rod, the rod can remain stationary on the inclined plane.

targets.text: Determine the direction and magnitude of the magnetic induction $B$ of a uniform magnetic field

If all other conditions remain unchanged, but the direction of the magnetic field is suddenly changed to vertically upward, and an external force $F$ is applied parallel to the rail direction to keep the metal rod stationary, determine the direction and magnitude of the external force $F$ acting on the metal rod.
